bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes humanitarian impact—using words like "lost," "rescue mission," and "went down"—and often frames the crash as part of a pattern of U.S. aircraft losses; right-leaning coverage amplifies threat with terms such as "crashes," " Iran War ," "vanishes," and "friendly airspace," foregrounding Iranian claims.
The left uses sympathetic, cautionary tones while the right favors alarmist, security‑first rhetoric; key divides include attribution, pattern‑of‑loss framing, crew counts , and economic fallout.
Both cite CENTCOM, stress rescue efforts and that one plane landed safely, linking shared facts to deeper ideological and geopolitical splits.
summary: On Thursday, U.S. Central Command said a U.S. KC-135 aerial tanker went down over western Iraq while another landed safely during Operation Epic Fury.
During Operation Epic Fury, the incident occurred in friendly airspace while CENTCOM said the crash was not the result of hostile or friendly fire.
Rescue teams are working and CENTCOM requested patience as officials gather details, saying it was unclear if there were casualties and noting at least five crew members were aboard.
The loss marks the KC-135 as the fourth publicly acknowledged U.S. aircraft lost during operations against Iran, following last week’s friendly-fire downings of three F-15s, CENTCOM said.
Amid the wider offensive that began on Feb. 28, the war has cost the United States $11.3 billion, killed seven U.S. troops, and wounded about 140 service members.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left coverage frames the event as a terrorism probe centered on the suspect's prior conviction for supporting ISIS and uses restrained descriptors like "convicted" and "terrorism probe," de-emphasizing immigration questions.

Right coverage foregrounds ideological motive and policy failure, using charged language—"ISIS-linked," "madman," "naturalized," "not deported," reports of "Allahu Akbar" and "sought out ROTC"—to amplify threat and accountability.
Center outlets use source‑attributed phrasing like "being investigated as" and praise "brave students.
Both sides agree on victims, the suspect’s ID and death, his prior conviction, and the FBI terrorism probe, reflecting security versus policy and cultural divides.
summary: An armed man opened fire inside Constant Hall just before 10:49 a.m. on March 12, 2026; one person was killed, two wounded, and the shooter was found dead.

According to the Justice Department, Mohamed Bailor Jalloh, 36, of Sterling, Virginia, pleaded guilty to attempting to provide material support to ISIS and was released from federal custody in December 2024.

Students intervened and subdued the shooter before law enforcement arrived, which FBI Director Kash Patel said 'undoubtedly saved lives'; police received calls at 10:43 a.m., arriving four minutes later, and an alert urged 'run-hide-fight protocols' near Constant Hall.

FBI Director Kash Patel said the FBI is investigating the shooting as terrorism with the Joint Terrorism Task Force, while ATF and FBI Norfolk agents support the response and two wounded U.S. Army victims were treated at Sentara Norfolk General Hospital.
Old Dominion's enrollment data show about 24,000 students, nearly 30% military-affiliated, while the gunman targeted ROTC students days before a weeklong school break beginning Monday.
title: Gunman in fatal Old Dominion University shooting identified as Mohamed Jalloh, a convicted ISIS supporter: Report

summary: On March 12, 2026, a vehicle crashed into Temple Israel in West Bloomfield and shots were fired; the suspected attacker was later reported dead at the scene.

Temple Israel operates an early childhood learning center and serves about 3,400 families, with staff completing FBI-led active shooter training weeks ago amid heightened alert related to the war in Iran.

A security guard was struck and knocked unconscious and later treated at a hospital, and the Temple Israel learning center was emptied with parents reuniting children at the Jewish Community Center, 6600 W Maple Rd, West Bloomfield Township, MI 48322.
Multiple agencies converged on the scene, with at least 80 police cars lining Walnut Lake Road, FBI agents and ATF personnel assisting local law enforcement, and nearby schools placed in secure mode.
Authorities are searching for another possible suspect while investigators work to identify the attacker, amid escalating Middle East tensions and a prior church attack five months earlier.
title: Suspect who attacked Michigan synagogue is dead and security guard injured, police say

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize a "chilling warning," stressing vows to "avenge" martyrs and the humanitarian and economic harm , whereas right-leaning outlets foreground doubts about authenticity and leadership—using words like "comatose," "alleged," and "mysteriously absent"—portraying the message as propaganda or a sign of regime weakness.
Center coverage frames a Strait of Hormuz closure as a "tool of pressure" and highlights market/geopolitical fallout.

Key dividing lines are the leader’s health/authorship, whether closure is leverage or an escalatory threat, and focus on human cost versus instability; all concur the statement was state-published and markets moved.
summary: On March 12, 2026, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei , Iran's Supreme Leader, vowed to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed and demanded U.S. military bases in the Middle East close, threatening attacks in his first public statement read on state television.
Following the Feb. 28 strikes, Khamenei was appointed on March 9 after his father was killed in U.S.-Israeli air strikes that started the conflict.
Citing retaliation, Khamenei said 'Iran will not refrain from avenging the blood of its martyrs,' and vowed to seek compensation or seize enemy property if refused.
Markets reacted as Brent crude oil rose 9% to more than $100 a barrel, and Iraq halted operations at all its oil terminals after a Basra port attack.

The U.N. refugee agency says up to 3.2 million people have been displaced in Iran and at least 759,000 in Lebanon, amid ongoing regional conflict, while sirens blared after midnight in Jerusalem and Iran-backed Hezbollah launched some 200 rockets from Lebanon at northern Israel.
title: Iran's new supreme leader vows to keep blocking Strait of Hormuz in first statement

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the release of 172 million barrels as a "180" degree turn, using language like "piggy bank" to highlight political inconsistency while focusing on "crushing" costs for families.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets portray the move as a strategic "turn" for leadership, using phrases like "hitting Iran hard" to link energy policy with military strength.
Center-Leaning reports de-emphasize political motives, instead highlighting "vulnerabilities" as the reserve hits 58% capacity.
While perspectives diverge on executive intent, all sides emphasize the coordinated global effort to stabilize prices that "rocketed" past $100 amid geopolitical conflict.
summary: Tapping the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, the U.S. will release 172 million barrels to help lower prices amid the Iran war, announced Wednesday.
Amid the Iran war, gasoline prices rose to about $3.58 per gallon, nearly 22% higher than last month when gas averaged about $2.94.

The SPR currently holds 415 million barrels, about 58% of the 714 million barrels authorized, and the IEA agreed to release 400 million barrels, the largest in its history, with U.S. participation up to President Trump, Burgum said.
Speaking to CNBC, Burgum added 'whether the U.S. participates is up to President Donald Trump , he'll make the final decision'.
Refilling the SPR, Trump said 'We'll do that, and then we'll fill it up,' indicating he plans to refill after the release.
title: Iran war: Trump will release 172 million barrels of oil from Strategic Petroleum Reserve

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the sanctions relief as a "gain" for Moscow, using skeptical language like "green light" to portray the move as an "unexpected injection of dollars" that weakens geopolitical pressure.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives emphasize "decisive steps" toward "stability," justifying the 30-day license as a pragmatic response to "Iranian threats" and "stranded" cargo.
While the center focuses on "pragmatic" market stabilization amid $100-per-barrel prices, the left highlights domestic political opposition.
Both sides acknowledge the Treasury’s 30-day window to curb energy costs, reflecting a shared tension between strict foreign policy and urgent global economic security.
summary: On March 12, the U.S. Treasury Department issued a 30-day licence allowing purchase of Russian crude and petroleum products stranded at sea, covering cargoes loaded by 12.01 a.m. Eastern time and valid until April 11, 12.01 a.m.

Escalating hostilities near the Strait of Hormuz have paralysed shipping after strikes and reprisals, with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps warning it would block shipments and Mojtaba Khamenei vowing to use the Strait as leverage.

Oil markets reacted, with Brent crude falling 0.38% to $100.10 and West Texas Intermediate dropping 0.58% to $95.17, while Bessent called the move a 'narrowly tailored, short-term measure' that 'will not provide significant financial benefit to the Russian government'.

There were about 124 million barrels of Russian-origin oil on water across 30 locations as at March 12, and the White House ordered the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation to offer political risk insurance while considering a Jones Act waiver.
Despite strategic releases including the U.S. Energy Department's 172 million barrels SPR plan, Saudi Arabia and UAE rerouting show no long-term replacement for barrels Iran kept off market.
title: U.S. allows temporary purchases of Russian oil already at sea to stabilize energy markets

bias: Right-Leaning coverage frames the story as a law-and-order outrage—blaming AB 3234/Newsom, spotlighting the 27 years served of a 355-to-life sentence, and using charged terms like "pedo," "vile," and "walk free" while detailing alleged conduct and admissions to amplify danger and demand board replacements; left-leaning attention is de-emphasized.
Center outlets instead use restrained institutional phrasing—"criticized," "pushing back," "convicted child predator"—and note the elderly parole program without broad political blame.
Key dividing lines are attribution to the governor/law, graphic offender details, and calls for political consequences; both sides agree the parole was granted and officials object.
summary: On Nov. 4, 2025, the California Board of Parole Hearings granted Gregory Vogelsang parole after serving 27 years of a 355-year sentence, prompting criticism from Sacramento County officials.
Under current state law, the elderly parole program allows inmates aged 50 or older with 20 consecutive years served to be eligible, a policy passed in 2017 and lowered from 60 to 50 in 2020.
Court records show Vogelsang was convicted on over 27 counts for molesting six boys in the 1990s, and he admitted luring victims before abuse at his parole hearing.

The Sacramento County District Attorney asked the California Board of Parole Hearings to re-review Vogelsang's release, with an en banc hearing scheduled for Wednesday, March 18, and urged the public to attend at 1515 K Street or call 916-267-0857.

Officials say victims face repeated trauma and therefore warn that an above-average risk assessment for reoffending prompts multiple bills to tighten elderly parole rules, including proposals to move eligibility back to 60.
title: Sacramento Officials Oppose Early Release of Convicted Child Predator Under Elder Parole Program

bias: Left coverage emphasizes worker hardship—using terms like "unpaid" and "obscene" and highlighting privatization and union warnings—framing "more than 260,000" DHS staff working without pay as a human-cost story.

Right coverage foregrounds partisan blame and security risk, deploying phrases like "Democrat shutdown," "donation drives," and "terror threats abounding," citing "over 50,000" affected to indict opponents.
Center reporting stresses practical wait-time trackers and guidance, de-emphasizing partisan frames.

Pivotal divides include the privatization debate and national-security framing; both sides nonetheless note longer lines, unpaid staff, and lawmakers venting frustration, reflecting broader ideological and cultural rifts.
summary: On Wednesday, March 11, 2026, Transportation Security Administration officers continued working without pay as TSA wait times climbed nationwide, clogging airport security lines.

Funding for the Department of Homeland Security lapsed in mid-February after Democratic lawmakers pressed for immigration enforcement changes, and Congress completed 11 of 12 appropriations bills this year except the DHS measure.
Major hubs and airport dashboards show variable waits, with the MyTSA mobile app reporting times up to 30 minutes and Houston's secondary airport facing lines over three hours.

Travelers are being urged to arrive earlier—TSA recommends two hours for domestic and three for international flights—and the U.S. Chamber of Commerce urges Congress to quickly fund DHS to ease system strain.

With the impasse entering its fourth week, Senate negotiators say offers have been exchanged but progress stalled, as Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., said the last White House offer came nearly two weeks ago.
title: TSA workers set to miss first paycheck with no end to shutdown in sight

bias: Left frames the story around humanitarian critique, using "migrants," highlighting protests and the "third batch" to stress repeated displacement; right frames it administratively, using "deportees," "receives" and number-focused reporting to normalize enforcement.
Center emphasizes secrecy, using "secretive" or "clandestine" and linking removals to the Trump administration while detailing nationalities.

Pivotal dividing lines include rights groups' "protest" and claims of clandestine operations versus emphasis on sovereign action; all sides agree on the arrival, batch count and that Eswatini confirmed the four deportees—revealing competing humanitarian versus sovereignty priorities rooted in broader ideological and geopolitical divides.
summary: Eswatini reported on Thursday that it had received four third-country deportees from the United States , raising the total sent there to at least 19 in the third batch.
The Trump administration used secretive third‑country agreements that sent more than 40 deportees to Africa and paid Eswatini $6.5 million to receive them.
The latest group included two Somali nationals, one Sudanese national and one Tanzanian national, while previous July deportees included nationals of Vietnam, Cuba, Laos and Yemen.

Local civic groups in Eswatini have protested amid political concerns, while local human rights lawyers appealed after last month's high court ruling and repatriations began, lawyer Alma David reported.

Intensive engagements with countries of origin are ongoing, and the Eswatini government reiterated it will uphold the rights and dignity of third‑country nationals detained in Eswatini despite some remaining in prison after serving sentences.
title: Eswatini Receives Third Batch of Migrants Deported by the US

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the dispute as systemic religious discrimination—using victim-centered, emotive terms like "excluded," "systematically," and "fierce legal battle," and highlighting the program’s $1 billion scale—while right-leaning pieces foreground the plaintiffs’ legal claim, quoting "blanket exclusion" and using adversarial wording such as "accuse" and "discrimination.
Center coverage is more procedural, using neutral verbs like "sue" and naming plaintiffs and the comptroller.

Pivotal divides include the program’s scale, the absence of an official rationale, and whether legal wording or community impact is emphasized; all sides agree federal suits allege exclusion from the voucher program, reflecting civil‑rights versus religious‑liberty tensions.
summary: Three private Islamic schools and three parents filed a federal lawsuit Wednesday challenging their exclusion from Texas's $1bn Education Freedom Accounts voucher program, alleging religious discrimination by state officials.

Acting Texas Comptroller Kelly Hancock, advised by Attorney General Ken Paxton, barred schools with ties to groups designated as foreign terrorist organizations; Gov. Greg Abbott defended the policy to prevent "radical Islamic indoctrination."

Plaintiffs argue they meet all neutral accreditation requirements, claiming officials "have systematically targeted Islamic schools for exclusion" based on categorical presumptions rather than evidence of unlawful conduct by Bayaan Academy or Houston Quran Academy Spring.

Families now face "deathly silence" and uncertainty as the March 17 application deadline approaches, fearing they will lose access to nearly $10,500 in annual public funding for their children's tuition.

The federal court must determine whether the state's vetting process violates constitutional rights or constitutes legitimate oversight, as plaintiffs seek an emergency injunction to pause the enrollment deadline during litigation.
title: Muslim parents, private schools sue Texas over exclusion of Islamic institutions in voucher program

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the defendant’s intent and casualty count—using terse legal framing like "wants to plead guilty" and "injured 5" and often labels him the "accused"—de-emphasizing name, weapon type and victim breakdown; right-leaning pieces foreground concrete details—identifying the 28‑year‑old, saying he "opened fire" with a "personal handgun" and noting four soldiers and one civilian—using vivid action language to heighten threat.
The pivotal dividing lines are suspect identification, weapon specifics and victim composition; center pieces mirror right’s concreteness.
Both sides, however, stress the plea and the five wounded, reflecting shared attention to legal consequences and public safety.
summary: Army sergeant Quornelius Radford, 28, is seeking to plead guilty to attempted murder and other charges for opening fire on co-workers at Fort Stewart in Georgia last August, injuring five.
Radford used a personal handgun to shoot four soldiers and a civilian worker who was his romantic partner before being disarmed by bystanders.
Army prosecutors have not offered a suspected motive and say Radford could still face a possible life sentence if the judge accepts his guilty plea.
title: Army sergeant wants to plead guilty in Georgia base shootings that injured 5

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the trade probe as a "revival" of Trump-era trade policy previously "struck down" by courts, context that is de-emphasized in other perspectives.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets portray the move as a "probe" into "unfair trade practices" by foreign adversaries.
While the left highlights a "failure" regarding human rights, the right focuses on economic sovereignty.
Center-Leaning reports remain technical, using "Section 301" to describe "fresh" investigations into 60 economies.
Both sides agree on the probe's scale and focus on forced labor, reflecting a broader geopolitical shift toward prioritizing domestic industry.
summary: On Thursday, the United States Trade Representative launched Section 301 investigations into 60 economies to determine whether they failed to ban imports of goods produced with forced labor.
Conducted under the Trade Act of 1974, these probes aim to replace reciprocal tariffs that the Supreme Court struck down last month, providing a new legal basis for potential duties.
USTR Jamieson Greer stated that American workers face unfair competition from foreign producers who gain an "artificial cost advantage" from the "scourge of forced labor."

Targeted nations include China, the European Union, India, Mexico, Japan, and Korea, with the office assessing whether their policies are "unreasonable" or "discriminatory" and restrict U.S. commerce.

Separately, President Donald Trump's administration initiated inquiries into 13 economies regarding "structural" excess capacity, while Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent prepares to meet his counterpart in Paris this weekend for trade talks.
title: U.S. launches fresh Section 301 probes into 60 economies over forced-labor trade practices

bias: Right-Leaning coverage frames the mission as a heroic "rescue effort," using action-oriented language like "jumped in" and "leads" to portray a proactive triumph.
Conversely, center-leaning reporting de-emphasizes this solo narrative, instead highlighting "collaboration" with the State Department and providing specific figures like "155 Americans" evacuated.
While the right employs escalatory terms like " Iran war " to signal geopolitical aggression, the center maintains a reportorial tone.
Left-Leaning perspectives are de-emphasized.
Both perspectives agree on the representative's partnership with a nonprofit, yet they diverge on whether to credit institutional support or individual conservative initiative.
summary: Recently, Republican Rep. Nancy Mace stayed in the Middle East and partnered with Tampa-based veteran-led Grey Bull Rescue to evacuate 155 Americans, including 11 infants, from Israel during its 808th mission.

After learning a South Carolina family was trapped amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, Mace worked with the U.S. Department of State to secure a plane when Grey Bull Rescue lacked transportation.
Operating since 2023, Grey Bull Rescue, a Tampa-based veteran-led crisis response group, has completed hundreds of missions, including its 808th to extract María Corina Machado in 2025.

The State Department reported over 43,000 Americans have safely returned since Feb. 28, and those needing help can register at step.state.gov or call the U.S. Department of State 24/7 at +1-202-501-4444.

Amid political debate, Republican Rep. Nancy Mace said 'When your people are stranded in a war zone, the only answer worthy of the office, worthy of our state, worthy of our country, is to get them out.
title: Nancy Mace says she flew to Middle East to get stranded Americans home

summary: Mexico considers Canada a fundamental part of the CUSMA trade agreement, emphasizing trilateral cooperation despite uncertainties from the U.S. administration.
The CUSMA agreement has helped protect Mexico and Canada from U.S. tariffs, including a 10% global duty on goods.
The U.S. is considering separate bilateral trade deals with Canada and Mexico amid uncertainty over the future of the CUSMA pact.
The CUSMA review in July could lead to renewal, withdrawal, or ongoing annual negotiations, with Mexico optimistic about maintaining a stronger trilateral agreement.
title: Mexican official says Canada is ‘a fundamental’ part of the CUSMA trade bloc

bias: While all sides frame the anchor as "legendary," coverage diverges in professional emphasis and emotional depth.
Left-Leaning outlets present a "beloved" figure through straightforward obituary-style reporting, de-emphasizing viral anecdotes.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives highlight his tenure as a conservative radio host, using "exclusive" accounts of "heartbroken" colleagues to foster sympathy.
Center-Leaning reports pivot toward "funniest moments," the "chicken" blooper, and provide medical context like "pneumonia.

Despite these shifts — from the Right’s focus on radio ties to the Center’s "optimistic spirit" — all perspectives unite in honoring his age 82 passing and nearly five-decade career as a New York "mainstay.".
summary: On Thursday, longtime New York City television news anchor Ernie Anastos died at age 82, his family confirmed to WABC; he was a staple of local broadcasting for more than 40 years.
Anastos began his career in Boston radio in the mid-1970s before pivoting to television, eventually earning more than 30 Emmy Awards and an Edward R. Murrow Award for broadcast excellence.

Over his 40-year career, Anastos covered major events including the September 11 attacks and the death of John Lennon, while later hosting "Positively Ernie" on WABC and creating the nationally syndicated show "Positively America."
WABC anchor Bill Ritter called Anastos "something unique" and "trusted" by viewers, while Fox 5 News honored his "lasting impact on New York journalism " and integrity in a statement.
Anastos remained active until recently, posting a final social media message on March 3 that championed truth in journalism—a commitment that defined his entire career.
title: Ernie Anastos, Legendary NYC News Anchor, Dies at 82

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes swift police action—repeating that a white Honda CR‑V was "recovered within hours" and framing it as effective work—using terms like "suspect vehicle"; right-leaning pieces stress public-safety takeaways, highlighting that "no one was injured" and reporting "two people" involved while criticizing authorities as "not providing any other details.
Center coverage is more cautious, favoring qualifiers such as "allegedly" and source attribution.
Dividing lines include injuries, number of occupants, and withheld details—items that trigger sharper partisan framing—yet all agree on the recovered stolen CR‑V and police as the primary source.
summary: On March 10, police say two male suspects exited a vehicle and fired multiple rounds at the United States consulate in downtown Toronto, around 4:29 a.m., before fleeing.
Police say the SUV was stolen shortly before the shooting, and Toronto police recovered a white Honda CR-V within hours near Queen Street W. and University Avenue.

Officials say no injuries were reported and people inside likely did not hear the gunfire because the consulate building is heavily fortified, with a damaged door and roughly 10 shell casings found outside.
RCMP and Toronto police are treating the case as a national security incident, while Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Mark Carney condemned the gunfire as an act of intimidation.

Investigators are searching for at least two suspects and two suspects remain outstanding, officials have not said whether the shooting is connected to recent targeted shootings at three GTA synagogues, and security at U.S. and Israeli consulates has been increased.
title: Vehicle sought in US consulate shooting investigation found, Toronto police say

bias: Left coverage emphasizes timing and preparation—using phrases like "set the stage"—portraying the Paris meetings as lead‑ins to Trump’s Beijing visit in concise, neutral tones; right coverage personalizes and foregrounds leaders and negotiation momentum—naming Scott Bessent , framing events as "trade talks" to "further discuss" ongoing negotiations and noting they occur "ahead of" the summit; center pieces include Greer’s "strong relationship" framing and goals to "stabilize" ties and address trade imbalances, noting Beijing’s silence—details de‑emphasized by left and right; all agree officials will meet in Paris less than three weeks before the summit, reflecting competing priorities of symbolism versus concrete economic aims.
summary: On March 15, senior U.S. and Chinese economic officials will meet in Paris, with U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent meeting Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer expected to attend ahead of President Donald Trump's March 31 to April 2 state visit.
The talks are meant to hammer out trade and economic achievements for the leaders' summit after a turbulent year of escalating tariffs and a one-year truce from Busan, South Korea.

Preparations are underway with private-sector hopes for positive outcomes as industry leaders and analysts watch for clues on possible U.S. purchases like soybeans and aircraft, US-China Business Council president Sean Stein urged ambition.
Bessent framed the talks as progress built on mutual respect and pledged to deliver results prioritizing America's farmers, workers and businesses.
Beijing has not confirmed the talks or the state visit, with Wang saying, 'This year is indeed a big year for China-US relations' as the planned visit would be the first since 2017.
title: Bessent will meet China’s vice premier in Paris ahead of Trump’s visit to Beijing

bias: Left coverage emphasizes a domestic media– politics conflict—reporting that the White House "denounced" CNN, that a Rapid Response team circulated a CNN clip and criticized the network, and that this was the second time in three days the administration singled out CNN, with some headlines using pejoratives like "psychotic," "melts down," and "attacking"; right coverage foregrounds the Iranian leader’s "threatening"/"hardline" tone, cites Strait of Hormuz risks and market fallout while de-emphasizing the denouncement angle; center pieces stress "denounces" and journalistic "responsibilities"; all agree a new leader spoke, revealing deeper divides between media‑politics and security/economic priorities.
summary: On Thursday, the White House criticized CNN for airing a portion of Iran's new supreme leader's public statement, the second time in three days, White House said.

White House communications director Steven Cheung earlier criticized CNN anchor Erin Burnett's interview with Seyed Hossein Mousavian, calling CNN a 'Total disgrace' for regurgitating unverified quotes, as President Donald Trump has long targeted the network.

Cable News Network showed an anchor reading Khamenei's remarks in Farsi with an English translation, while Sky News, Al Jazeera, and The Associated Press also circulated the content; an X account with his portrait posted the text in both languages.
Media observers warned that airing the remarks requires caution, saying CNN responded to the White House attack on Thursday, and American journalists must take care reporting wartime perspectives.

Platform-first: an X account posted Farsi and English versions of Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei's remarks after he succeeded his father, who was killed in an Israeli air strike, while Paramount Global's purchase of Warner Bros. Discovery raised editorial independence concerns.
title: Trump Administration Denounces CNN for Airing Messages From Iranian Leaders

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the story as skeptical and accountability-driven—emphasizing the FBI "warning," labeling Newsom as having "dismissed" or merely being "aware"—while right-leaning pieces stress national-security stakes, foregrounding Iran as an external danger and using words like "deny," "imminent threat," and "destruction" to suggest officials may be minimizing risk; centrist coverage is more hedged, repeating "no imminent threat" and balancing institutional reassurances.

The key divides are how seriously to take the FBI alert and whether the governor’s response is dismissive or reassuring; both sides, however, report the warning and the official "no imminent threat," reflecting deeper ideological splits over security and trust.
summary: Newsom, speaking Wednesday, March 12, 2026, said there was no imminent threat despite an FBI warning about Iran-linked drones .
The FBI recently warned police departments about a possible California strike tied to Iran, describing an alleged UAV plot from an unidentified vessel off the U.S. coast as unverified.

Police in Los Angeles and San Francisco said they were monitoring world events for risks and coordinating with state and federal authorities, while an FBI spokesperson posted the alert on X after an ABC News report.
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on X the tip was based on 'unverified intelligence' and wrote Thursday, 'No such threat from Iran to our homeland exists, and it never did.'

Officials framed the response as a posture of preparedness, with Brian Kohlhepp saying special information bulletins from the FBI commonly raise awareness for local law enforcement rather than demand immediate action.
title: California governor says no imminent threat despite warning about possible Iran drone attack

bias: Left-Leaning pieces emphasize human cost and moral urgency, framing a catalogue as a "grim list" and saying the Michigan attack "resurrected fears," while right-leaning coverage stresses reassurance, repeatedly calling worship "remarkably safe" and de-emphasizing broader alarm; center coverage bridges both—labeling the incident an "ambush" and a "spate" while supplying statistical context.
Rhetorically the left uses emotive, victim-focused language, the right uses concise, calming phrasing, and the center blends vivid labels with numbers.
Dividing lines include statistical framing, the "ambush" characterization, and absence of casualty or perpetrator details; all sides nonetheless report the attack and heightened anxiety.
summary: Across countries, the inventory highlights incidents including the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh, Christchurch mosques in New Zealand, and the First Baptist Church in Sutherland Springs, Texas, since 2012.

Investigations attribute motives ranging from white‑supremacist and antisemitic ideology, as seen in the Sikh Temple attack and the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh, to Islamist extremist motives, including the Sinai mosque attack.
Attackers employed shootings, vehicle rammings, stabbings, bombings, Molotov cocktails and arson, including a pickup truck attack and arson at a Michigan church recently.
Courts and governments have responded with long sentences and policy changes, and some attacks have intensified fear among clergy and worshippers worldwide.

Despite the scale of attendance, fatalities from attacks on individual houses of worship remain below a few hundred annually, though recent incidents cited in 2024–2025 have intensified fear among clergy and worshippers worldwide.
title: Some notable attacks on houses of worship around the world in recent years

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes a hurried, multi‑state "rush"—using phrases like "rush/rushing" and "penny problem"—portraying legislatures as imposing ad‑hoc, patchwork fixes, while right-leaning pieces frame passage as an accomplishment—using terms like "clears" and "addresses the impacts"—and spotlight a single‑state win .

A key dividing line is the U.S. Mint's 3.7‑cent production cost: center and right cite it to justify the halt, left largely de‑emphasizes it; center also foregrounds committee actions and lighthearted puns.
Both agree production stopped, shortages exist, and rounding laws are spreading—differences map onto familiar government‑action versus deregulation framings.
summary: On March 10, 2026, the Florida Legislature passed SB 1074 allowing retailers to round cash transactions to the nearest nickel, approving it 111-1 under Sen. Don Gaetz's sponsorship before sending it to Gov. Ron DeSantis.

After the federal order in February 2025, President Donald Trump directed the U.S. Department of the Treasury to stop producing pennies, costing 3.7 cents each, while circulating roughly 114 billion existing pennies.

Under the measure, symmetrical rounding dictates totals ending in 1, 2, 6 or 7 cents round down, while those ending in 3, 4, 8 or 9 cents round up, with examples like $1.92 becoming $1.90 and $1.99 becoming $2.

Rounding will be optional for retailers and applies only to cash payments, so electronic transactions and card purchases remain unchanged; Rep. Angela Nixon opposed the measure, preferring automatic rounding down to protect cash purchasers.

At the federal level, lawmakers have proposed the Common Cents Act, with researchers warning that pricing patterns could favor businesses, and the Treasury says stopping penny production saves $56 million annually.
title: State lawmakers rush to set rounding rules for when there are no pennies

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the promotion as occurring "amid probe" and stresses the "abrupt resignation," highlighting accountability and potential scandal, while right-leaning pieces emphasize trustees "moved quickly" or "acting swiftly" to elevate the provost—portraying decisiveness and stability; center coverage notes bypassing the traditional nationwide search and relays the board chair that the board "wants what's best for the university.
Rhetorically, the left uses suspicious, investigative language; the right uses reassuring, action-oriented terms.

Pivotal divides are the existence of a probe and the bypassed search; both sides nonetheless agree on the appointment and the sudden departure, reflecting deeper tensions between institutional scrutiny and governance priorities.
summary: On Thursday, Ohio State University's Board of Trustees is expected to appoint Executive Vice President and Provost Ravi Bellamkonda as the institution's 18th full-time president at a 9 a.m. meeting.

Former President Ted Carter resigned Monday after admitting to an "inappropriate relationship" with someone seeking state resources for her private business, prompting the Board's acceptance of his departure.
JobsOhio invested $15,000 per episode in "The Callout," a podcast hosted by Krisanthe Vlachos that featured Carter as a guest and is now under review regarding his departure.

Earlier this week, the Ohio Conference of the American Association of University Professors criticized the university, labeling the leadership selection a "secretive process" lacking meaningful inclusion of students and faculty.

Bellamkonda, formerly the Vinik Dean of Engineering at Duke University, previously led initiatives at Emory University and currently oversees Ohio State's academic enterprise, including AI programs for nearly 67,000 students.
title: Ohio State names provost as its new president after predecessor’s abrupt resignation

bias: Left coverage frames the moment as a cynical, undeserved honor—using tags like "smugly" and "didn't earn"—while right coverage foregrounds the giver’s motives and symbolism, naming the Order of Ikkos "prestigious" and calling the athlete a "legend" who is "aligning with MAGA ," "opens up" about IVF and support for women’s sports; left moralizes Trump's acceptance, right humanizes and celebrates it with words like "surprises" and "gifted.
Pivotal divides include whether Humphries' motives and the award’s prestige matter versus whether the honor was merited; a neutral outlet uses plain, descriptive language.
Both sides nonetheless agree the medal was presented, Trump accepted it, and the moment was emotional, a split that maps onto wider ideological and cultural fault lines.
summary: On Thursday, Kaillie Armbruster Humphries presented her Order of Ikkos to President Donald Trump at the White House, saying, 'I believe this actually makes you the first president in history to ever be awarded an Order of Ikkos.'
Drawing on Olympic tradition, Humphries said she chose to recognize Trump to spotlight his positions on women's athletics and family issues.

After winning two bronze medals at the Milano Cortina 2026 Olympics, Humphries described her achievement as meaningful after returning from IVF and health issues, and thanked Trump for supporting family-building policies.

President Donald Trump responded from the stage and appeared pleased after Humphries announced she selected him, with Humphries saying she believes this makes him the first president to receive an Order of Ikkos.

By thanking Trump for policies on women's sports and IVF, Humphries said, 'We only get one, but I want to recognize the support and impact you've had on women's sports throughout the Olympic movement, specifically standing up to keep biological women in women's sports, to keep the field of play safe, and allow for fair competition', likely drawing public attention.
title: US Olympic bobsledder surprises Trump, awards him prestigious Order of Ikkos medal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes corporate malfeasance and moral outrage—foregrounding phrases like "robbing them blind" and "so stupid" and labeling the conduct "cartoon‑villain level" price‑gouging—portraying the unsealed Slack messages as evidence of systemic consumer harm.
Right-Leaning pieces also use charged terms but frame the story as proof the DOJ settlement was premature, highlighting Republican AGs who "blast" the deal.
Center coverage is more procedural, treating the messages as a complicating wrinkle to the settlement and trial.
All sides agree the messages were unsealed and have prompted state and federal scrutiny, reflecting deeper divides over regulation versus market power.
summary: On Wednesday, court filings showed Judge Arun Subramanian ordered the release of 2022 Slack messages between Ben Baker and Jeff Weinhold in the case, after Live Nation argued they were irrelevant.
The U.S. Department of Justice antitrust division filed its 2024 lawsuit against Live Nation, which sought to keep Slack exchanges sealed, claiming they were irrelevant and informal banter.

Ben Baker, regional ticketing director, wrote 'I gouge them on ancil prices', boasting about charging $50 to park in the grass and $60 for closer grass while calling concertgoers 'these people are so stupid'.

The settlement requires Live Nation to pay $200 million, open Ticketmaster tech to third parties, limit exclusivity, and cap amphitheater fees at 15%, but over two dozen states rejected it as inadequate.

Plaintiff states argued the messages show Live Nation monetizes its amphitheater monopoly by imposing fees that degrade fan experience, and if unresolved, the trial could resume next week with ongoing state actions.
title: Live Nation employee mocks customers as ‘so stupid’ in internal messages released in court case

bias: Left outlets frame Netanyahu’s remarks as escalation, foregrounding a "veiled threat," Iran’s alleged Strait of Hormuz blockade vow and contested leader-death claims, using accusatory verbs like "threatens"; right outlets valorize strength, using terms such as "swaggers," "crushing," and metaphors like "no life insurance," and emphasize Lebanese casualty numbers as proof of effectiveness; center coverage de-emphasizes partisan spin, focusing on his defense of the "joint assault.

Pivotal divides are whether the rhetoric is reckless or deterrent and the credibility of leader-death/blockade claims; all still report his vows, the joint campaign, and that Iran is "no longer the same.".
summary: Benjamin Netanyahu , standing between two Israeli flags, said on March 12, 2026, he issued a veiled threat to Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei and vowed to keep striking Hezbollah after their March 2 attack.
On March 12, 2026, the U.S.-Israeli air campaign has struck Iran, targeting its Revolutionary Guards Corps and Basij paramilitary force, Israel says, to eliminate Tehran's nuclear and missile threat.

Netanyahu said 'I will not detail the actions we are taking... a regime is toppled from the inside' and added 'But we can definitely help and we are helping'; he also noted daily talks with U.S. President Donald Trump.

On March 12, 2026, Israeli warplanes bombed Beirut overnight after evacuation warnings, striking near the prime minister's office, the UN building, and foreign embassies, while sirens blared across Israel.

Netanyahu called Mojtaba Khamenei a 'puppet of the Revolutionary Guards' and said he 'cannot show his face in public' on Thursday, while Iran's new Supreme Leader's comments were read on state TV, vowing to keep the Strait of Hormuz shut.
title: Netanyahu Threatens Iran New Leader, Says Government Collapse Uncertain
Day 13: Oil Tankers Hit in Iraq, Pressure on Hormuz, Brent Above $100

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the cancellation as a moral failing, foregrounding human-rights terms like "torture camp" and "notorious" and portraying the dropping of indictments against five soldiers as evidence of impunity; right-leaning narratives emphasize procedural defenses—"ambiguous footage," "evidentiary difficulties," "dropped charges"—portraying the outcome as legally justified and accusing critics of smear or leak-driven hysteria while noting the detainee's release to Gaza complicated prosecution.
Pivotal divides center on the leaked video, the detainee's release, and government rhetoric like "blood libel.
Center coverage de-emphasized moralizing and stressed legal review; all agree charges were dropped, video exists, and public reaction was intense.
summary: On March 12, 2026, the Israeli military announced it is dropping charges against five soldiers accused of sexually abusing a Palestinian detainee, according to The Associated Press.
At Sde Teiman, a detention facility for Palestinians rounded up from Gaza, a leaked Channel 12 video last year purported to show abuse, prompting fury and ultranationalist overruns.
Members of Israel’s far-right government called the investigation an affront to their service, and the uproar led to the resignation last year of the military’s top legal official amid the Gaza war.
Critics argue the decision reinforces long-standing accusations that Israel fails to hold soldiers accused of crimes against Palestinians, deepening domestic division.

Internationally, the story highlighted Israel’s claim that its forces act within military and international law, with Channel 12’s leaked footage and The Associated Press coverage drawing wider scrutiny.
title: Israeli Military Drops Charges Against Soldiers Charged with Abusing Palestinian Prisoner

bias: Left outlets frame the story as speculative, highlighting Trump's hedging—'probably'—and the pejorative 'damaged' to underscore uncertainty, while right outlets amplify and legitimize his claim, foregrounding 'probably alive', 'damaged', ties to 'Operation Epic Fury' and tags like 'lightweight' or 'Dead Or Alive?' to dramatize impact.

Center coverage leans investigative/sensational—asking 'Is he in a coma? Has he lost a leg?' and noting a 'cardboard cutout.' The pivotal split is whether Trump's words are treated as proof of a strike or partisan speculation; all agree Khamenei remains unseen and reporting is dominated by conjecture.
summary: On Thursday, President Donald Trump stated he believes Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei is "probably alive" but "damaged" during an interview on "The Brian Kilmeade Show."
Mojtaba Khamenei assumed leadership after his father was killed in U.S.-Israel strikes on February 28; the 56-year-old cleric has not appeared publicly since his selection two weeks ago.
Diplomat Alireza Salarian claimed the leader is hospitalized with injuries to his "legs and hand and arm," though Iranian officials maintain he is "safe and recovering.
title: Trump Says Iran’s New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei Is Probably Alive but Injured

summary: On Wednesday, Anthropic sought a stay from a U.S. appeals court of the Pentagon's "supply-chain risk" designation pending judicial review after the Department of Defense barred its AI products, and it filed a separate lawsuit earlier this week in California.
After weeks of dispute over guardrails on military use, Anthropic resisted giving the Pentagon unrestricted access to Claude, seeking guarantees on surveillance and autonomous-weapons limits.
In filings, Anthropic said more than 100 enterprise customers contacted the company and lawyers warned of potential lost revenue in 2026 reaching billions of dollars.

The Pentagon action canceled a $200 million Pentagon contract signed in July 2025, allowing OpenAI to fill the void while AWS, Google Cloud, Azure and defense contractors may have to certify zero exposure to Anthropic.
title: Anthropic seeks appeals court stay of Pentagon supply-chain risk designation

bias: Left outlets frame the story as a corrective, accusatory narrative—using labels like "FACT FOCUS" and terms such as "falsely claiming" and "mischaracterization," and spotlighting the administration's suggestion that mail-in ballots "can lead to fraud"—while right-leaning coverage, though it also uses "falsely claiming," is much briefer and de-emphasized on report details and Carter's recent views.

The center emphasizes context—Carter’s 2020–24 support and a motive to push "proof-of-citizenship/photo ID" rules—making the pivotal divides whether Carter’s later statements and alleged legislative aims are foregrounded; all agree the administration cited the 2005 commission and dispute its characterization.
summary: The 2005 Carter-Baker report on elections found absentee ballots to be the largest source of potential voter fraud, but did not discourage vote-by-mail overall and offered recommendations to reduce risks.
Former President Jimmy Carter endorsed mail-in voting and absentee ballots in 2020, saying they protect "the core of American democracy — the right of our citizens to vote".
Around 30% of voters cast mail-in ballots in the 2024 U.S. election won by Trump, higher than pre-pandemic levels, including in Republican-controlled states like Indiana and South Dakota.
title: Trump Administration Misrepresents 2005 Carter-Baker Report on Mail-In Voting

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the move as a judicial correction, using terms like "rebukes" and "illegally" to stress accountability, while right-leaning pieces cast the judge as an "activist judge" and celebrate the administration's action as it "derails" the ruling in a breathless "JUST IN" tone—deploying triumphant, combative language; center reporting labels the agency "diminished" and says the nomination comes "amid controversy," noting Rogers' critiques of European misinformation laws.
The clearest dividing lines are how the judge and Kari Lake's conduct are characterized and the agency's stature, even as all report the nomination and the court's invalidation.
summary: On Thursday, President Donald Trump nominated Sarah B. Rogers, Under Secretary of State for Public Diplomacy and Public Affairs, to lead the U.S. Agency for Global Media and appointed Michael Rigas, Deputy Secretary of State for Management and Resources, as acting CEO.
On Saturday, U.S. District Court Judge Royce Lamberth ruled Lake's appointment invalid, voiding her actions including mass layoffs, as she was not a Senate-confirmed official per the Vacancies Act.

VOA staffers welcomed the court submission but said they needed clarity on agency leadership as the three plaintiffs and attorneys for the government confirmed no acting CEO served before the new appointments.
It is unclear if Sarah B. Rogers will keep her State Department role while leading USAGM, and the department has not clarified that point, with staff awaiting operational plans.
On Thursday, the court ruled that Kari Lake, acting as de facto CEO, led USAGM in violation of law, reducing VOA to a shell; she remains deputy CEO.
title: Trump nominates State official to replace Kari Lake at VOA parent agency

bias: Left outlets frame the ruling as evidence of partisan harm—using charged terms like "gerrymandered" and "targeting" and spotlighting the split of Kansas City and threats to the 5th District—while right outlets portray the decision as a win, using phrases such as "poised to gain," "validated," and noting the map was "backed by Trump" to signal legitimacy and advantage .
Left coverage is accusatory and civic-rights–focused; right is triumphalist and electoral.
Center coverage "GOP-friendly"/"upheld" emphasizes procedure and appeals.
Both sides agree the judge kept the map in effect and that appeals loom, reflecting deeper partisan battles over representation versus power.
summary: On Thursday, Jackson County Circuit Judge Adam Caine denied plaintiffs' request to block the 2025 Plan, finding they failed to meet the 'heavy burden' and ruling the map complies with Missouri's requirements.
Following a September special legislative session, Republican lawmakers approved the redistricting under pressure from President Donald Trump , and Gov. Mike Kehoe signed the map into law.
The map splits Kansas City into three districts, uses Troost Avenue to divide the 4th and 5th districts, and reduces split municipalities from 30 to 13.

If the map stands, it is likely to send one more Republican to Missouri's U.S. House delegation, and the ruling arrives about five months before the Missouri primary, setting up a tough race for Emanuel Cleaver II against multiple Republican primary challengers.

With appeals pending, a challenge over whether mid‑decade redistricting is constitutional is before the Missouri Supreme Court, while opponents submitted more than 300,000 signatures in December and said they will appeal Caine's ruling.
title: Missouri judge rejects a challenge to new US House districts backed by Trump

bias: Coverage splits sharply: left/center pieces frame the story as a punitive "junk food crackdown" and cast beneficiaries as "fight back" plaintiffs emphasizing the lawsuit's procedural claim under the Administrative Procedure Act and a multi‑state scope, while right‑leaning pieces spotlight "restrictions," name administrative actors and dates, stress items like "sugary drinks, candy, desserts" and invoke "Make America Healthy Again" to justify public‑health aims.
Rhetorically, left/center use adversarial, empathetic language; right leans on liberty, regulatory‑overreach diction and specificity.
Both note waivers, the lawsuit and health‑impact claims, reflecting deeper ideological divides over paternalism versus personal freedom.
summary: On March 11, five SNAP recipients sued the U.S. Department of Agriculture in federal court in Washington, D.C., seeking to halt SNAP waivers approved in 22 states that block sugary drinks and candy purchases.

USDA began processing the waivers last year as part of a Trump administration push to ban candy and processed foods, endorsed by Brooke Rollins and Robert F. Kennedy Jr. as part of the Make America Healthy Again movement.
The complaint details both legal and personal impacts, alleging USDA violated the Administrative Procedure Act and citing Amanda Johnson's autistic daughter losing six safe foods.
The filing asks a court to immediately block implementation and void the waivers, potentially affecting SNAP caseload: 42.1 million average per month in 2023, or 12.6% of the U.S. population.

Plaintiffs warn the waivers destabilize food access and cause checkout confusion, forcing SNAP participants and families to choose between restricted items and essentials, while supporters argue limits could encourage healthier diets.
title: Food stamp recipients sue USDA over restrictions on candy, energy drinks

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the Costco lawsuit as a quest for consumer justice, using language like "deserve" and "owed" to emphasize that shoppers are entitled to a "slice" of refunds.
They highlight the risk of the retailer being "paid twice" by both customers and the government.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage portrays the action as an aggressive legal "demand," focusing on allegations that the company is "illegally holding" funds.

While the center highlights an "avalanche" of litigation and a potential $166 billion industry-wide impact, both sides agree the Supreme Court’s ruling on Trump-era tariffs triggered this conflict over corporate profit versus consumer restitution.
summary: On Tuesday, Costco shopper Matthew Stockov filed a proposed nationwide class action in Illinois alleging the company must return tariff refunds to customers, calling retained funds 'unjust enrichment'.

Earlier this month, the U.S. Supreme Court struck down tariffs, creating a pool of roughly $166 billion in refunds owed to more than 330,000 importers with over 53 million entries, according to CBP data from March 4.

Speaking to investors this month, Costco chief executive Ron Vachris said it is unclear 'what refunds, if any, will be received' and noted Costco membership fees bring in 73% of Costco's operating income.
CBP is developing a new refund system to streamline tariff returns, the U.S. Court of International Trade ordered refunds, and FedEx has pledged to pass on funds to affected parties within 45 days.

The scale of litigation and agency limits suggest more than 1,000 companies have filed suits in the U.S. Court of International Trade, while CBP officials warned that administrative procedures are ill suited for refunds.
title: Costco sued by shopper in potential tariff class action case

summary: On Thursday, the International Criminal Court Office of the Prosecutor dropped its investigation, announcing the United States did not commit crimes against humanity with Venezuela sanctions.
In 2020, Venezuela's government alleged that U.S. sanctions since 2014 were 'unlawful coercive measures' causing suffering, murder, deportation, and persecution, as part of Venezuela II.

The Office of the Prosecutor said evidence must provide a reasonable basis to believe U.S. sanctions led to alleged crimes, but it did not find the evidential requirements of causation and intent were met.

The court noted the Venezuela II closure is separate from the ongoing Venezuela I investigation and unrelated to the January 2026 events in Venezuela; separately, the prosecutor's office is advancing a Belarus probe.
Appeals judges last year ordered chief prosecutor Karim Khan recused from a Venezuela probe, and Khan is currently on leave amid sexual misconduct allegations.
title: International court drops investigation into US sanctions on Venezuela
